<div slot="header">

**39 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Hi yes, you are correct in that I am also using the taskList variable name, but from what I heard from peers, the correct naming convention is tasks instead of tasklist

Seems like convention is to write it as tasks, instead of taskList.
• taskList is redundent since the type alr shows it is a list
• taskList can be used if differentiating from taskQueue or taskSet

Haven't gotten around to changing mine, but feel free to take this suggestion in as well
</panel>

### 4. LIM ..NETT `@Whimsyturtle` (32 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756519055" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think you should replace this with `Donk User Guide` 😆 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756520450" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you can consider adding `data/donk.txt` to `.gitignore`?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756522847" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Similar to README.md, I believe you should replace this with `Donk` 😅 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756531433" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Following the Java coding standard, I believe that for JavaDoc comments, there should be "Punctuation behind each parameter description." (i.e. "List of tasks to save." instead of "List of tasks to save")
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756532258" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Good job on grouping similar constants together, and using SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756533933" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you can consider reusing `createStorageFile()` here?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756545720" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you could have a similar logic to `Deadline` class, where it supports both String and DateTime values? Currently, it seems that `Event` class does not format the DateTime value in `toString()`?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756549042" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you can move the logic of `throw new StorageException(...)` into `Storage` class instead?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756556907" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Following the Java coding standard, I believe we should use Egyptian style brackets, i.e.
'''java
if (command == null) ``{``
    continue;
``}``
...
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756562983" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Following the Java coding standard, I believe we should use Egyptian style brackets, i.e.
'''java
public boolean isExit() ``{``
    return false;
``}``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756568137" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you can consider declaring this as a constant variable of the class?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756574121" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you can consider using the pre-existing Java `IllegalArgumentException` (https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/lang/IllegalArgumentException.html)? Unless you want to make an  Exception that's specific to Donk?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756579152" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you could move the declaration of `sb` to _after_ the `if (matchingTasks.length == 0) ``{``...``}``` conditional, so the variable's scope will be minimal?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756583714" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you could move this ASCII art out into a public constant in `Donk` class, and reference it here?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/97#discussion_r2756734955" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Same for all the other JavaDoc comments in other classes.
</panel>

<div slot="header">

<div slot="header">

<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Typically in java we use a method to do something.
So in this case, in terms of completeness, using a complete phrase `markTask` instead of a `mark` may be better?

Also note that a single `mark` is at the risk of being taken as a noun, rather than a verb, if without the object in the phrase 😺 

Same for `unmark` method.
</panel>

String[] commandParts = remaining.split("/from", 2);
// handle missing /from
commandParts = remaining.split("/to", 2);
// handle missing /to and continue
'''

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/147#discussion_r2754448603" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

According to the coding standard, I think the Javadoc comments should look something like this:

'''suggestion
    /**
     * Updates taskList with Task object
     * Prints message for user indicating task being added
     *
     * @param toAdd Task being added
     */
'''

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/182#discussion_r2762628848" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As for me, I think the current method name is fine. There's an nice consistency in the `markAs&lt;status>` format.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/182#discussion_r2762648304" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Although programmatically there is an established convention for the `to&lt;Something>` _(and it's something I use outside 2103 too)_, I don't think it counts as a verb in standard English so it might pose an issue in Code Quality > Naming.
Perhaps it's worth modifying it to something like `convertToStorageString` just to eliminate any ambiguity so you don't have to worry about the naming violations?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/182#pullrequestreview-3749297480" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Excellently done - besides very minor nitpicks, I think this PR is squeaky clean when it comes to `Coding Quality > Naming`. Awesome stuff!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762178759" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed (after seeing other classes) that the headers comments sometimes start with a capitilised word, while others are fully in lowercase. I'm not sure what's the preference but I think it'd be cleaner to stick to one.

While lowercase starting words are not at all disallowed in the coding standard, I feel that each sentence in the header comments should start with a capital letter to better differentiate between a sentence wrap and a new sentence.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762220661" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> You MUST write header comments for all classes, public methods. But they can be omitted for the following cases:
> i. Getters/setters
> ii. When overriding methods (provided the parent method's Javadoc applies exactly as is to the overridden method)
> iii. In classes/methods used for testing

I may be wrong, but I think this method requires header comments too since it's a public method. I do agree that this method logically doesn't need explanation _(since all Java apps must have it)_, but perhaps something simple like "Main entry point for the app" would fulfill the coding standard
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762244916" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Scratch variables used for temporary storage or indices can be kept short. A programmer reading such variables should be able to assume that its value is not used outside a few lines of code. Common scratch variables for integers are i, j, k, m, n and for characters c and d.

It's not explicitly disallowed from coding standards, but I feel that there is a connotation that single-letter names should be used for primitives/iterator scratch variables that don't hold much importance. I would consider the Scanner to hold enough significance since this one instance grabs all user inputs. Besides, although the scope is quite small, it's not _that_ small (~20 lines compared to, say, a 5-line for loop).

Maybe a slightly longer name would be better? This one is more personal though so it may not count as a coding standard issue.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762275982" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think this public constructor is missing header comments. Just like the `main` method entry point, I do agree this is quite silly, but I think something like "Returns a new instance of &lt;Class>" along with parameter documentation should suffice 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762305342" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Oooo, I love the indentation alignment you did for the param section. Maybe consider doing this for other header comments too? _(This isn't in the coding standards requirements haha, I just really dig it)_
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762312849" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Ooop this isn't part of the coding standard checks but I think you missed a space after the colon, there are other lines down there with the same issue if that's the case
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762322528" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> In method header comments, the first sentence should start in the form Returns ..., Sends ..., Adds ... etc. (not Return or Returning etc.)

Maybe you missed the 's' in ensure&lt;ins>**s**&lt;/ins>, there are a couple in other methods I've noticed too like "load" 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762339967" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Variables with a large scope should have long names, variables with a small scope can have short names.

Perhaps a slightly longer name like `dueDate` will be better since this has quite the large (global) scope
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762346188" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I believe this is a constant, so it should be in SCREAMING_SNAKE case. Maybe something like `TASK_PREFIX`? Similarly for the other task classes.
_(While you're at it, you may considering adding `final static` since it's shared between all Deadline instances. This isn't a coding standard issue though)_
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2762369081" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

> Plural form should be used on names representing a collection of objects.

Personally I think "log" as a noun is an apt name but to hit the scope-to-naming-length and the plural naming criteria, perhaps change it to `allTasks`?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#pullrequestreview-3748956417" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**18 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Awesome stuff! There were just a few nitpicks here and there and some stuff that you probably accidentally missed, but most of the commits follow the coding standards well.

Also, after doing the entire review, I now realise that there were some missing header comments probably because you already hit the quota and just stopped - just ignore my comment about that if that's so.
</panel>

</panel>

**1 :fas-comment:**

As per Java coding standards, the Javadoc comment should start with a verb. You can refer to the addressbook2 repo example, class comments start with "Represents..", so perhaps you can try "Represents a task with a due date" ?

I notice that you've done this for a lot of your class descriptions, so for each instance, I shall indicate a SWV (Start with verb)
</panel>

While this does work for switch case, you could consider either changing your format to a lamda style or, since this is expected to return a string, you could initialise an empty string before the switch-case, then for each case, that string variable will store the return value, and you'd only have to return once at the end. This would mean that there would be an explicit break statement at each case, making the code a bit more readable.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2770530549" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It seems like the standards for getters for booleans is "isDone". According to Google, you can either
1. Have both the field and method be named isDone.
2. Have the field named done and the method named isDone.

The former seems to comply with this course's coding standard the most:

> As much as possible, use a prefix such as is, has, was, etc. for boolean variable/method names so that linters can automatically verify that this style rule is being followed.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/393#discussion_r2770537838" expanded>

'''suggestion
        if (parts.length &lt; 2) ``{``
            throw new KiraException("OOPS! The description of an event cannot be empty.");
        ``}``
        String[] eParts = parts[1].split(" /from ");
        if (eParts.length &lt; 2) ``{``
            throw new KiraException("OOPS! Please add a start time (use /from).");
        ``}``
        String[] timeParts = eParts[1].split(" /to ");
        if (timeParts.length &lt; 2) ``{``
            throw new KiraException("OOPS! Please add an end time (use /to).");
        ``}``
'''

<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

'''suggestion
    /**
     * Loads tasks from the storage file.
     *
     * @return An ArrayList of Tasks loaded from the file.
     * @throws KiraException If the file format is corrupted or cannot be read.
     */
'''

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/345#discussion_r2754789966" expanded>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/345#pullrequestreview-3739927889" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, the main work you need to do right now is to break your program into multiple files. This is no longer Python toy programming, and as you work on more complicated software engineering projects it will no longer be possible to write everything into one file. Do have more descriptive variable names as well! Other than that, the code is generally fine on first glance. 😄 
</panel>

</panel>

<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/161#discussion_r2772730295" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/161#discussion_r2772735133" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

For the error message here "Error saving tasks: ", could we use a slightly more neutral phrasing like "Unable to save task list: " to remain more professional for users?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/161#discussion_r2772740787" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like the "_toDo_" as a noun for the variable name and "_execute_" as the verb naming for the method, which follows our naming guidelines and clear to understand.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/161#discussion_r2772744754" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Regarding the private method "_saveList_", since I noticed this exact same logic in AddDeadlineCommand and AddEventCommand, would it be more efficient to extract this method and store it into a shared parent class(eg.Storage class) directly?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/161#pullrequestreview-3761290700" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

I like how you have consistently followed the coding standard like using nouns for classes (eg. AddTodoCommand, AddEventCommand) and verbs for methods (eg. execute, isExit). Which makes the codebase very clear and much easier for future project expansion. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/211#discussion_r2771983412" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Here the task is retrieved using _tasks.get(num - 1)_ before the bounds check _if (num &lt;= tasks.size() && num > 0)_. Would it be safer to perform the bounds check first to avoid potential IndexOutOfBoundsException?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/211#discussion_r2771992430" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Since the DeleteCommand is actually expecting a numeric index, any reason that you choose to take num as a String here and parse it in the execute method? Maybe can parse it inside the Parser class, so this class can be more focused on execution.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/211#discussion_r2772000742" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like how you did the check to ensure the end date is after the start date, it avoid the potential logical error which having inconsistent tasks added to the list.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/211#discussion_r2772003623" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Regarding the error message: "only write /from and /to once please!", maybe phrasing like "The input should contain exactly one /from and one /to tag" would sound clearer for users?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/211#discussion_r2772006714" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like how you did a check for an empty task list at here. This is a good way to handle those edge cases before loop through the _tasks_.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/211#pullrequestreview-3760455240" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

I have left some suggestions in the files, you can check them out.
Overall, glad to see great progress on these increaments!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/346#discussion_r2801991544" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

This method has weak SLAP, it has high-level file iteration with low-level like split string and index based parsing (taskLine[3]). Could consider to abstract the logic (convert a single line text into a Task object) into a separate helper method.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/346#discussion_r2801998861" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

This section (while -&gt; try -&gt; switch -&gt; if) exhibits arrow-head style code. You can try reduce this by extract out the switch logic into a separate method.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/346#discussion_r2802004820" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**16 :fas-comment:**
</div>

For switch case, theres no indentation for switch and the case. So eg:
'''java
switch (argsArr[0]) ``{``
case "T":
   arr.addTask(Todo.loadTodo(argsArr));
   break;
case "E":
   arr.addTask(Event.loadEvent(argsArr));
   break;
case "D":
   arr.addTask(Deadline.loadDeadline(argsArr));
   break;
default:
   throw new JaneException("Oh dear, an error has occurred\n");
``}``
'''
</panel>

While understood as an alias for `load()`, also returning the same type and throwing the same exception, could this method be omitted and simply `load()` be used instead? The method `loadTask()` is used only once in the main app, and it is also the only time `load()` is invoked (via `loadTask()`), so there might not be a need for aliasing currently.
</panel>

**11 :fas-comment:**

In your `TaskListTest.java`, you initialize tasks as `todoTask`, `deadlineTask`, `eventTask`. Because of that logic, I think the attribute 'task' could be called 'name', 'taskName' or 'description' . Minor point: I feel that preferably the longer `indexToMark`, or `markedIndex` might be easier to understand?
Right now "index" in `indexMark` sounds like a verb ("to mark an index"), but this variable a noun (the index itself). Because usually the second word is the main point of the name (variable is specifically an "index" instead of a "mark" here)

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall good consistency and clarity in naming. Some minor inconsistencies as pointed out in the comments. Some names are shortened, eg `cmd` and `arg`, but I think they are generally still understandable, so it's up to you if you think spelling them out in full would benefit others reading your code.
Also extra point about packaging: currently all your classes are packaged into one big `sunshine` package, maybe it would be easier for others to find your different classes if you grouped them further by functionality eg. `Main`, `Tasks`, `Utility`?
Overall good work!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/387#discussion_r2772359161" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like the use of all the other methods in this class to prevent direct access and modification of the private attribute `tasks`! Would it be more consistent to also return a copy of `tasks` instead of the reference itself to prevent modification of `tasks` by `Storage.save()`?

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/249#discussion_r2764789327" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**17 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I see many repeats of the line:
"if (index &lt; 0 || index >= tasks.size()) ``{``
            throw new NimbusException("Task number is out of range.");"
To improve readability and scalability, maybe you can extract it to a helper method as such:
private void validateIndex(int index) throws NimbusException ``{``
    if (index &lt; 0 || index >= tasks.size()) ``{``
        throw new NimbusException("Task number is out of range.");
    ``}``
``}``
Hope this helps! 😄 
</panel>

<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It might be good to move the data folder to another location, since src/main/java is typically only used for source code.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/262#discussion_r2775261342" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As per the SE coding standards, operators should be at the start of a new line instead of the end.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/262#discussion_r2775277036" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

When there are many tasks, string concatenation can degrade performance. It might be better to use `StringBuilder` instead.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/262#discussion_r2775284561" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It might be a good idea to split the commands into their own classes, so the logic of each command can be easily modified in the future.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/262#discussion_r2775288784" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It might be better to import individually, especially since there is only 1 file in mortis/storage.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/262#discussion_r2775295592" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the variable name should be `FILE_PATH` since it's a constant.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/262#pullrequestreview-3764332421" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Good design for the tasks! Having different classes for each task type makes it easy to add new types in the future.

I think it would be even better if each command is made into its own class too. This way, modifying the logic of existing commands and adding new ones would be easy.

Furthermore, I think some of the code is violating SE coding standards, and the data folder should be moved from the src folder.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#discussion_r2775213589" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think the data file should not be placed inside src/main/java, as these are supposed to be for source code only.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#discussion_r2775218229" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It would be better not to push generated binary files.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#discussion_r2775220604" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Good catch! Making the commands case-insensitive would make it less frustrating for the users.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#discussion_r2775225116" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think this class should be in another file, and it can be made into a regular class.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#discussion_r2775229062" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Currently, I think this is acceptable. But in the future, it might be beneficial if the user is informed that there are corrupted tasks (e.g. how many there are, which ones).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#discussion_r2775234469" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think Task and its subclasses should be put in their own files to reduce clutter.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/447#pullrequestreview-3764296091" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Good design! I like how you split the tasks into different classes. It makes it easy to add new types of tasks in the future. However, I think you can make it neater by splitting them into different files too.

Furthermore, splitting the commands into different classes (e.g. AddTodoCommand, MarkCommand, etc.) would be beneficial for future extensions.

Also, it might be better not to push the .class files, because we generally don't push generated binary files to repositories.
</panel>

</panel>

### 67. VINC..ANTO `@VincentJulijanto` (15 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772573143" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

add a space before ``{`` to match typical Java style: ... throws IllegalArgumentException ``{``
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772577855" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

input.split(" ") only splits on single spaces and can create empty tokens if there are multiple spaces. Consider using input.trim().split("\\s+") for more consistent parsing and cleaner code.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772588443" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Suggestion: this switch block is getting quite long and handles multiple responsibilities (validation, parsing, storage updates, and response formatting). Consider extracting each command’s logic into smaller helper methods to improve readability and maintainability
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772595472" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

getStatus() returns a boolean, so a name like isDone() may better follow Java naming conventions for boolean getters
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772603364" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

consider making fields private instead of protected and exposing them through getters/setters if needed. This improves encapsulation and keeps class state better controlled
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772611380" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Nice use of inheritance here, delegating common fields to Task keeps Todo simple and focused
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#discussion_r2772614598" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

consider extracting the date format pattern into a constant (e.g., private static final DateTimeFormatter DISPLAY_FORMAT = ...) to avoid recreating the formatter each time
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/216#pullrequestreview-3761136809" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, the structure is clear and the task abstraction is well designed. The separation between Task, Todo, Deadline, and Event makes the code easier to extend in future features. The error handling in Steve.getResponse() is also comprehensive. Some methods could be further decomposed to improve readability, but overall the implementation is clean and understandable
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#discussion_r2772641097" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The parseTask method currently handles multiple responsibilities such as command dispatching and validation. You might consider separating the command selection logic from the parsing logic into smaller helper methods or a command handler structure to improve readability and extensibility
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#discussion_r2772643205" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Nice abstraction with DateTimeParts and parseUserDateTime. This keeps the parsing logic modular. If the application grows, you might consider moving date/time parsing into a dedicated utility class
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#discussion_r2772657714" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

getDescription() is protected, which helps preserve encapsulation while still 
allowing subclasses to access it. This is a good design choice
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#discussion_r2772665668" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

toDataString() hardcodes the storage format ("T | ...") inside Task, while subclasses (if any later) may need different prefixes/fields. Consider making toDataString() overridable (or abstract) and letting subclasses decide their own serialization format, to avoid “format knowledge” being spread across multiple classes later. This also makes adding Deadline/Event easier without modifying base class logic
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#discussion_r2772670820" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Consider validating the index before accessing the list to provide a clearer error message instead of relying on IndexOutOfBoundsException from ArrayList. This could improve robustness and user feedback consistency
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#discussion_r2772675708" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Nice use of copying the input list (new ArrayList&lt;>(tasks)) to avoid exposing the original reference. This helps maintain encapsulation of the internal task list
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/266#pullrequestreview-3761213675" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**15 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, the code structure is clear and readable, with good separation of concerns between Parser, Task, and TaskList. Error handling in the Parser is especially helpful for guiding user input.

Some minor suggestions were left regarding readability, encapsulation, and maintainability, but overall the implementation looks solid and consistent.
</panel>

</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/223#pullrequestreview-3751984917" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764015967" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the import statements aren't following the conventional ordering. Currently, the static import on line 3 appears after regular imports. Could you reorganize them like this?
```
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

import java.time.LocalDateTime;

import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;

import kiko.storage.Storage;
import kiko.task.Deadline;
import kiko.task.Event;
import kiko.task.Task;
import kiko.task.Todo;
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764024617" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the static import is using a wildcard (*). Imports should always be listed explicitly rather than using wildcards. Could you update this to explicitly import only the assertion methods you're actually using in the test?

```
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertTrue;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertFalse;
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764036456" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the import ordering needs to be corrected according to the coding standard. Currently, the imports are mixed up. Could you reorganize them like this?

```
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertTrue;

import java.io.File;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.nio.file.Files;
import java.nio.file.Paths;
import java.time.LocalDateTime;

import org.junit.jupiter.api.AfterEach;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.BeforeEach;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;

import kiko.task.Task;
import kiko.tasklist.TaskList;
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764047762" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I really like how clean and well-documented this class is! The Javadoc comments are clear and each method has descriptive documentation that explains its purpose clearly.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764060857" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the case clauses are indented. According to the coding standard, case clauses should not be indented - they should be at the same level as the switch keyword itself. Only the statements within each case should be indented. Could you adjust the indentation to follow this format?
```
switch (command) ``{``
case LIST:
    handleList(taskList, ui);
    break;

case MARK:
    handleMark(taskList, argument, ui, parser);
    break;

// Following code
```
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764072953" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The inline comment ```// Default behavior - create a Todo task``` repeats what's already in the Javadoc above. Could this be removed to reduce redundancy?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/280#discussion_r2764083542" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**14 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

```

    statements;
``}``
```

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/92#discussion_r2779215868" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The sole purpose of this getter is for Storage.java to call it to iterate through its tasks directly (!); Is there perhaps a way to iterate and prepare the String within TaskList.java to protect encapsulation?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/92#discussion_r2779223955" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

```
public String getStatus() ``{``
        return isDone ? "[X]" : "[ ]";
    ``}``
```

I like how clean this is. Though I do wonder if things may get out of hand (have to write and maintain many many methods) if somewhere in the future, your task has lots of information about it (And many corresponding parts of the String to output).

Also, since your method already returns isDone, perhaps the String representations logic can all be moved to toString, for easier maintainability. In fact, toString is the only place this method is called: why not remove the method and move all the logic there and store it in a variable?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/92#discussion_r2779234681" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

All of <mark, unmark, delete, (etc.)> have this same repeated code which checks if the index exceeds tasks.size(), and later, TaskNotFoundException. I wonder if they can be generalized to have this code in just one place?


```
try ``{``
            if (index > tasks.size()) ``{``
                throw new TaskNotFoundException();
            ``}``
```

```
catch (TaskNotFoundException e) ``{``
            System.out.println(e.getMessage());
            return (e.getMessage());
        ``}``
```
</panel>

### 95. ZHEN..AWEN `@incursi0n` (13 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#discussion_r2753976117" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The name parts is quite generic. Would something like `commandAndArgs` make it clearer that the first element is the command word and the second is the rest of the input? That might help when reading the switch and the rest of the method.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#discussion_r2753984535" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Would a name like `lineFields` make it clearer that these are the parsed fields of a single line, rather than generic `parts`?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#discussion_r2753991805" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps you may consider something like `dueDateTime` for consistency with other date/time variable names (e.g. start, end in Event) rather than a generic variable name such as `by`?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#discussion_r2753996260" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I notice that `parts` is reused after each split() for different meanings (first “command + rest”, then “description + from/to”, then “start + end”). Could introducing separate variables for each stage (e.g. descriptionAndTimeRange, then startString and endString) make the parsing flow easier to follow and avoid misleading the reader about what parts[0] and parts[1] represent at each step?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#discussion_r2754002302" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like that you use a distinct name for the multi-valued result (foundTasks) rather than reusing tasks. it matches the “distinguish single-valued and multi-valued” rule in the guideline and makes the method easy to read. 😊
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#discussion_r2754007032" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

We have `printMarkTaskMessage`, `printUnmarkTaskMessage`, and `printDeletedTask`. For consistency with the “message” wording, did you consider `printDeletedTaskMessage` so it’s clear we’re printing the “task deleted” message rather than “a deleted task” in general?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/228#pullrequestreview-3738955440" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

I think that most of the naming conventions are align with the guideline. Good work with the code overall! 👍
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/399#discussion_r2752941593" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the variable name `seperator`. Could this be a typo for `separator`? Also, since this value doesn’t change, would you consider defining it as a named constant in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE (e.g. `SEPARATOR`) to match the [Java coding standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html) for constants?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/399#discussion_r2752958737" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

This looks like it might be debug output. Would it be better to remove it before merging so the user doesn’t see the raw date string printed?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/399#discussion_r2752977992" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Good job for using debugging prints to test the code! However, should this be extracted out of the commit?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/399#discussion_r2752979153" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The Java coding standard says class names should be nouns. Often we use singular forms for class names (e.g. `Todo`, `Event`, `Deadline`) since each class describes one kind of entity. Was there a reason you went with the plural forms here? I’m curious whether singular might make the naming a bit more consistent with the rest of the codebase.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/399#discussion_r2752995046" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**12 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps use a space before ```{``` in `catch`? The style guide suggests a space before the opening brace. So maybe you could use ```}`` catch (NumberFormatException e) ``{``` instead of ```}`` catch (NumberFormatException e)``{```? I noticed the same issue in several other places too.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/399#pullrequestreview-3737735655" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**13 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Great job overall! I like how you have followed most of the SE-EDU standard. 😊
</panel>

</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2768972217" expanded>

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2768994792" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2769008125" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**

To align with the real that logical units within a block should be separated by one blank line, would it be more intuitive to add a space between the `dialogContainer.getChildren().addAll(...)` call and the userInput.clear() call? It helps to separate the output and input reset logic. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2769013629" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**

</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, this a very solid PR! The code is clear and easy to understand. It also follows the coding standards very closely. I have left a few minor suggestions regarding constant extraction and code formatting to fully align with CS2103's standards. Great work done so far!
</panel>

</panel>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/457#discussion_r2763781707" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed that arr[0] is used directly here even though taskType has already been defined. I think using taskType.equalsIgnoreCase(...) might improve readability and keep the code more consistent.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/457#discussion_r2763832809" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Should the boundary check here be "idx >= 0 && idx &lt; list.size()"?

I think there may be a potential off-by-one issue in the index boundary check here.

let's say our command is "mark 4" and assume now we have 3 tasks in the task list. Then list.size() will give us 3, and idx = 4 - 1 = 3, so 0 &lt;= 3 && 3 &lt;= 3, which will give us true. Then the program execute list.get(3)... This will out of list's boundary
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/457#discussion_r2763845108" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

</panel>

### 119. CHEN..PING `@p12010304` (13 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/136#discussion_r2767157615" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In Deadline.java, dateTime is a bit generic. Since the context of a deadline is specifically when something is due, naming it by or deadlineTime would be more descriptive and align better with your toString output ((by: ...))
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/136#discussion_r2767159310" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The name getFileString() describes the implementation (returning a string for a file) rather than the intent. Since you are essentially formatting the task for storage, something like toStorageString() or toFileFormat() might be more intuitive. It tells the reader why this string exists.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/136#discussion_r2767178169" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Following the 'Use Searchable Names' guideline, consider renaming s to something like taskData and c to taskType. Single-letter variables like s make it harder to search the codebase and don't convey the intent of the data being held.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/136#discussion_r2767180151" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Using hardcoded indices like substring(2) or substring(5) can be risky and is a form of 'hidden' naming. It would be cleaner to define these as constants, e.g., private static final int BY_PREFIX_LENGTH = 3;. This makes it clear why you are skipping those specific characters.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/136#discussion_r2767183618" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In most standard libraries, find implies returning a single element. Since this returns a subset of tasks, filterTasksByKeyword or searchTasks might be more intuitive names that clearly describe the operation's result.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/136#pullrequestreview-3754611505" expanded>

<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/267#discussion_r2759867605" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The switch case could be following the format as per the se-education link
```
switch (condition) ``{``
case ABC:
    statements;
    // Fallthrough
case DEF:
    statements;
    break;
case XYZ:
    statements;
    break;
default:
    statements;
    break;
``}``
```
</panel>

This entire CommandHandler class currently acts as a 'God Object' by handling parsing, execution, and UI logic. ### 139. NG Z..VIER `@javnz1` (12 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#discussion_r2759164066" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like that the class has a clear Javadoc explaining the support task types, this makes it very clear to the readers.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#discussion_r2759179292" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on the coding standard, the if statement irregardless if there is only one statement, it should still be wrapped around with the curly braces like the following suggestion:
'''suggestion
            if (arr[i].equals(token)) ``{``
                return i;
            ``}``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#discussion_r2759185793" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on coding standard you should avoid using single line 'if statement', and instead wrap with curly braces like the following suggestion:
'''suggestion
        if (start >= endExclusive) ``{``
            return "";
        ``}``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#discussion_r2759193791" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Based on coding standard the switch statement should not have indentation between the switch () and case : 


'''suggestion
        switch (taskType) ``{``

        case "todo" -&gt; ``{``
                String description = join(args, 1, args.length);
                if (description.isEmpty()) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException("The description of a todo cannot be empty! >w&lt;");
                ``}``
                task = new TodoTask(description);
            ``}``

        case "deadline" -&gt; ``{``
                int byIdx = findToken(args, 1, "/by");
                if (byIdx == -1) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException("Please provide a description and deadline using '/by'! >w&lt;");
                ``}``

                String taskDescription = join(args, 1, byIdx);
                String byTime = join(args, byIdx + 1, args.length);

                if (taskDescription.isEmpty() || byTime.isEmpty()) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException("The description or deadline of the deadline cannot be empty! >w&lt;");
                ``}``

                LocalDate byDate = parseInputDate(byTime);
                if (byDate == null) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException(">w&lt;! I don't know this format: \"" + byTime + "\". "
                            + "Please use something like: Jan 28 2026 or 2026-01-28 &hat;w&hat;");
                ``}``
                task = new DeadlineTask(taskDescription, byDate);
            ``}``

        case "event" -&gt; ``{``
                int fromIdx = findToken(args, 1, "/from");
                int toIdx = findToken(args, 1, "/to");

                if (fromIdx == -1 || toIdx == -1 || fromIdx >= toIdx) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException("Please provide event description and time using '/from' and '/to'! >w&lt;");
                ``}``

                String eventDescription = join(args, 1, fromIdx);
                String fromTime = join(args, fromIdx + 1, toIdx);
                String toTime = join(args, toIdx + 1, args.length);

                if (eventDescription.isEmpty() || fromTime.isEmpty() || toTime.isEmpty()) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException("The description, from time, and to time of the event cannot be empty! >w&lt;");
                ``}``

                LocalDate fromDate = parseInputDate(fromTime);
                LocalDate toDate = parseInputDate(toTime);

                if (fromDate == null) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException(">w&lt;! Pookie doesn't know this format: \"" + fromTime + "\". "
                            + "Please use something like: Jan 28 2026 or 2026-01-28 &hat;w&hat;");
                ``}``

                if (toDate == null) ``{``
                    throw new PookieException(">w&lt;! Pookie doesn't don't know this format: \"" + toTime + "\". "
                            + "Please use something like: Jan 28 2026 or 2026-01-28 &hat;w&hat;");
                ``}``

                task = new EventTask(eventDescription, fromDate, toDate);
            ``}``

        default -&gt; ``{``
                throw new PookieException("Pookie doesn't know the task: " + taskType + ". Please use 'todo', 'deadline', or 'event'. ;w;");
            ``}``
        ``}``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#discussion_r2759210138" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**

I like how the package name is all lowercase and follows the project based naming, which aligns with the **_Naming -&gt; Packages_** rule
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#discussion_r2759262915" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**

I like how you have created an abstract Command class so that it can serve as a base for child class inheritance like your AddCommand, DeleteCommand, ExitCommand etc.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/151#pullrequestreview-3745365222" expanded>

Great work pookie! 👍 💯

<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**

Perhaps you could place a line break before the operator and use an 8-space indentation for the wrapped line to improve readability?

<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You use return; to exit the loop when the user types "bye". While this works, have you considered using a boolean flag (e.g., isExit)? It often makes the loop termination condition more explicit at the top of the while block.
</panel>

cs2103t convention is that "Conditionals need to be put on a separate line" and "Single statement conditionals still need to be wrapped with curlies".

'''suggestion
if (remainder.isBlank()) ``{``
    throw new TanjyException("You did not indicate which task to mark!");
``}``

if (!parts[1].matches("\\d+")) ``{``
    throw new TanjyException("That's not a number :((");
``}``
'''

This also applies to the "if" statements in the unmark, todo, deadline, event and delete commands.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/157#discussion_r2763852303" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**

The error checking, parsing of DateTime, Event Adding and UI printing could be separated by one blank line with comments to improve readability. Same for "deadline" and "delete".

'''suggestion

// Error Checking
if (remainder.isBlank()) ``{``
    throw new TanjyException("Event description cannot be empty.");
``}``

details = remainder.split("/from", 2);
if (details.length == 1) ``{``
    throw new TanjyException("You need to set a start date by adding '/from' after the task!");
``}``

String[] timeRange = details[1].split("/to", 2);
if (timeRange.length == 1) ``{``
    throw new TanjyException("You need to set an end date by adding '/to' after '/from'!");
``}``

// Parsing DateTime data
LocalDateTime from = parser.parseDateTime(timeRange[0].trim());
LocalDateTime to = parser.parseDateTime(timeRange[1].trim());

// Adding Event to TaskList
taskList.addEvent(details[0].trim(), from, to);

// Printing success
ui.printAddSuccess(taskList.getMostRecentTask(), taskList.size());
'''
</panel>

### 170. TAN ..RMAN `@shermszz` (12 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/107#discussion_r2764031053" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

"I noticed that most classes (Ui, Parser, Storage) are currently located in the single ```egg``` package. As the project grows, would it be beneficial to group them into logical sub-packages (e.g., ```egg.ui```, ```egg.parser```, `egg.task`)? This often helps with separation of concerns and access control.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/107#discussion_r2764041805" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I really like how you extracted the commands into their own classes (```AddCommand```, ```DeleteCommand```, etc.). This makes the ```Parser``` much cleaner and adheres well to the Single Responsibility Principle. Nice refactoring.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/107#discussion_r2764069073" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the variable is named `by`. Since variable names should generally be nouns to describe what the object is (rather than a preposition describing the relationship), would a more descriptive name like `deadline`, `dueDate`, or `completionDate` be better here? This would also avoid potential confusion with the `/by` delimiter keyword.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/107#discussion_r2764078065" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed these variables are named `from` and `to`. According to our naming guidelines, variables should be nouns (describing what the value is) rather than prepositions.
Additionally, `from` and `to` can be ambiguous (referring to location, time, or people). Would more descriptive noun-phrases like `startDate` and `endDate` (or `startTime`/`endTime`) make the code more self-explanatory?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/107#pullrequestreview-3751079416" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/434#discussion_r2763907100" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed that the package folder is named KJ (starting with an uppercase letter). According to the Java Coding Standard, package names should be in **all lower case** (e.g., kj) to distinguish them from classes. I suggest you rename this package to adhere to the convention to improve readability and navigation as your project expands later on. You could rename it to `kj`, and the same for all your other subfolders with `kj.command` etc...

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/434#discussion_r2763927859" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like how you have already organized the Command class as an abstract class, and modularising all your other command logic into other classes that will override your execute method. This separation of concerns is great and makes the project structure much cleaner. Good job
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/434#discussion_r2763931786" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/434#discussion_r2763942728" expanded>

### 178. CHEN..NHUA `@e-hua` (12 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/193#discussion_r2752873925" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Not necessarily a code quality issue. 
But it would be better if you removed the indentations for the cases in this switch statement. 

For your reference: [No indentation for case clauses](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#package-and-import-statements:~:text=The%20switch%20statement,this%20style%20instead.)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/193#discussion_r2752920745" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Perhaps a bit more specific for a class-level field like this? 

'''suggestion
    private Task taskToAdd;
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/193#discussion_r2752932738" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The prefix `d` here feels a bit like 'texting-style' spelling, perhaps a name like this? 

[Implementation → Code Quality → Naming → Basic → Use standard words](https://nus-cs2103-ay2526-s2.github.io/website/schedule/week4/topics.html#w4-6-code-quality-naming:~:text=Use%20correct%20spelling%20in%20names.%20Avoid%20%27texting%2Dstyle%27%20spelling)

'''suggestion
            String[] deadlineParts = details.split(" /by ");
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/193#discussion_r2752947898" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The method name`setStatus` sounds a bit vague. A more descriptive name, such as `setDone`, might be better?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/193#discussion_r2752966134" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Local variables like this can also be more specific, such as `Task taskDeleted = tasks.delete(index)`.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/193#pullrequestreview-3737661652" expanded>

**6 :fas-comment:**

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Decent work on this PR! The inheritance of the Command abstract class is clear and intuitive, and the test cases are well-organized—great job overall.🔥🔥
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/272#discussion_r2752603826" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Not sure I like the way you named the setter of the boolean field here, perhaps rename this to `setDone()`?

[Setter methods for boolean variables must be of the form:](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#:~:text=Setter%20methods%20for%20boolean%20variables%20must%20be%20of%20the%20form%3A)

'''suggestion
    public void setDone(boolean isDone) ``{``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/272#discussion_r2752674850" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Any reason why you named the class `UI` instead of `Ui` like the standard below?


[Abbreviations and acronyms should not be uppercase when used as a part of a name.](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#loops:~:text=Abbreviations%20and%20acronyms%20should%20not%20be%20uppercase%20when%20used%20as%20a%20part%20of%20a%20name.)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/272#discussion_r2752686597" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

It might be a good idea to import the packages from `java.util` explicitly, which serves as documentation and improves the readability of the import list.

[Imported classes should always be listed explicitly.](https://se-education.org/guides/guidelines/PRs-reviewing.html)
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/272#discussion_r2752704372" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Agreed! Maybe follow the [Java coding standard](https://se-education.org/guides/conventions/java/intermediate.html#package-and-import-statements:~:text=The%20switch%20statement,this%20style%20instead) here to avoid deep nesting.

### 193. NGUY..HINH `@natsupercell` (12 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/168#pullrequestreview-3759235447" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Very clean and well-structured code!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772605368" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think a better way to intepret `"_".repeat(100)` is by storing it as a static final field (so that you may reuse it over and over again without breaking abstraction)
for example
`private static final String LINING = "_".repeat(100);`
by doing this later on if you decide to change the lining format into something else (for aesthetic reasons for example) you only need to change the LINING field, not the whole code
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772615082" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

according to this course's convention, `switch` and `case` should be indented on the same level
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772621584" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think a better practice to handle repeatedly used constants like this (in this case, the message error) is to assign it to a constant variable. By doing so, if you want to change anything, you only need to change the variable, not the copies in the code.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772672010" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

```
toAdd = String.join(";", values);
fw.write(System.lineSeparator() + toAdd);
```

Why don't you write these 2 lines outside of the if else clauses? That would make the code less lengthy and more manageable. For example, if those 2 lines cause some bug then you only need to change those 2 lines, not the copies.

For example this is how I would write it:

```
String[] values;
for (Task t : updatedTaskList) ``{``
    if (t instanceof Todo) ``{``
        values = ``{``Type.TODO.getCode(), (t.getDone()) ? "1" : "0", t.getDescription()``}``;
    ``}`` else if (t instanceof Deadline) ``{``
        values = ``{``Type.DEADLINE.getCode(), (t.getDone()) ? "1" : "0", t.getDescription(),
                ((Deadline) t).getDeadline().format(Parser.dateAndTime)``}``;
    ``}`` else if (t instanceof Event) ``{``
        values = ``{``Type.DEADLINE.getCode(), (t.getDone()) ? "1" : "0", t.getDescription(),
                ((Event) t).getStart().format(Parser.dateAndTime), ((Event) t).getEnd().format(Parser.dateAndTime)``}``;
    ``}``
    toAdd = String.join(";", values);
    fw.write(System.lineSeparator() + toAdd);
``}``
```


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772687190" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Great use of enum, I want to learn from this.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772689719" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Sentences in javadoc comments should end with a period
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772705030" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Why don't you make the letter 'D' a Task's field? For example when you implement inheriting classes you can reassign the field with 'D', 'E' or 'T' (based on the subtype) during instantiation.
This is a good OOP practice as it keeps the abstraction.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#discussion_r2772725231" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I suggest you implement the logic in corresponding classes (Deadline and Event), not in this function. 
The reason is that if you do it like this you have to extract private information from classes, which violate "tell, don't ask" principle.
Distributing the work to the Deadline and Event classes makes the code much more manageable.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/364#pullrequestreview-3761172799" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

**11 :fas-comment:**

static final fields (constants) must be in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE

for example, I suggest you rename "byeMessage" to BYE_MESSAGE
</panel>

</div>

### 213. TIAN..ARUI `@Tian-Jiarui` (12 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/100#discussion_r2767759885" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Could this line be too long? Perhaps there could be more line indentations. I like to use String.format to make my String.toString() more future-proof too! I noticed the same way of writing in other places too. 😄 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/100#discussion_r2767795296" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Should there be indentations between the cases? I recall the java coding standards to recommend no indentation for case clauses.

Java coding standard reference:
switch (condition) ``{``
case ABC:
    statements;
    // Fallthrough
case DEF:
    statements;
    break;
case XYZ:
    statements;
    break;
default:
    statements;
    break;
``}``

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/100#discussion_r2767806285" expanded>

<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** CHANGES_REQUESTED

The code logic seems solid, but I noticed a systemic issue with Package Naming Conventions.

All packages currently use PascalCase, but standard Java convention requires packages to be all lowercase (e.g., messaging, inputhandling, tasks).
</panel>

</panel>

The ui parameter is never used in any of the implementation in the subclasses, but rather the static methods of the Ui class are used. Would it be better to remove this parameter instead?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/112#discussion_r2762428402" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Even though they are declared static final, the PrintWriter and BufferedReader objects are not constants as their contents are mutable, and thus should not be declared with all caps. Refer to https://google.github.io/styleguide/javaguide.html#s5.2.4-constant-names.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/112#discussion_r2762432167" expanded>

### 232. ZHU ..NGZE `@bitxer` (11 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/358#discussion_r2752629315" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Since you included java doc for the other constructor, I would suggest to include it for this as well so as to highlight what the difference is
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/358#discussion_r2752634845" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The constructor here is similar to the overloaded constructor below. Critically, I noticed that your error message is not printed for both despite the similar reason. This can cause confusion to users

I would suggest `this` is used to call the overloaded constructor instead. For example, something like the following would work:

'''java
public Deadline(String description, String by) ``{``
    this(description, by, null);
``}``
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/358#discussion_r2752733698" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**

<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Class variables should never be declared public. It is recommended to declare this as `private` explicitly
</panel>

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed that multiple `*.class` files are included despite it being in `.gitignore`. This is usually because you updated your `.gitignore` after committing. I would suggest that the `*.class` files are removed

### 245. KEE ..SONG `@KeeZhiSong` (10 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/93#discussion_r2766974703" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

All command files have consistent Javadoc coverage. Keep up the good work!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/93#discussion_r2766984427" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

FORMAT_CMD is declared as package-private (static final without an access modifier), while every other command class declares it as private static final. Consider adding private to match the rest.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/93#discussion_r2766996274" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Missing @param tags in constructor Javadoc
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/93#discussion_r2767001977" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

There's a stray " at the end of the @throws line. This is present in both DeadlineCmd and EventCmd (and ToDoCmd line 36). Per the guide, Javadoc should have clean punctuation - a period, not a dangling quote.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/93#pullrequestreview-3754393578" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/309#discussion_r2767043442" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

protected fields violate encapsulation rule. While protected is technically non-public, these fields already have getters (getDescription(), isDone()), and a setter (markAsDone(), unmarkAsDone()). Making them private would be more consistent with the spirit of the rule, since subclasses don't directly access these fields anyway.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/309#discussion_r2767047465" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Import ordering: static imports should come before non-static.
The correct order is:

import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertThrows;

import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/309#discussion_r2767049943" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Catching generic Exception instead of specific types. Per the guide and general Java conventions, catching Exception is overly broad. This could mask unexpected bugs. It should catch AeolianException | IllegalArgumentException specifically, which are the only exceptions parseTaskLine declares.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/309#discussion_r2767051878" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Consistent and thorough Javadoc coverage. Every single public class, constructor, and method has Javadoc with proper @param, @return, and @throws tags.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/309#pullrequestreview-3754468659" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**10 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

LGTM. Keep up the good work!
</panel>

### 247. CHEW..ACIA `@gracias022` (10 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/311#discussion_r2773100374" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Good adherence to the code-quality naming guidelines. Class and variable names are clear and noun-based (`Ui`, `scanner`), while methods start with verbs (`readCommand`, `showLine`). The names are descriptive without being overly long, avoid abbreviations or ambiguous terms, and generally explain intent well. Methods with similar purposes are named similarly (`getGoodbyeMessage`, `getUnmarkMessage`, `getMatchingTasksMessage`) which is good.

However, the descriptive comments above each method should be converted to Javadoc comments (/** ... */) since they document the respective method’s behavior.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/311#discussion_r2773242033" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed that the Ui class currently handles both display and task state changes for mark/unmark. You might consider invoking the task state changes in another class, e.g. a Command class, to adhere to the Single Responsibility principle and improve future maintainability. 

Also, if you wish to customise error messages for IndexOutOfBoundsException, you could check for index bounds in mark/unmark or before calling mark/unmark, or specifically handle this exception. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/311#discussion_r2773316197" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

`savedTasks` is currently static, which couples all Holiday instances to the same storage file. You might consider making it an instance field instead
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/311#discussion_r2773894097" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think this test could be improved to check that the loaded tasks match the expected tasks

parseIndex() assumes parts[1] always exists and is numeric. You may want to validate missing/invalid indices (and check for &lt;= 0 / out-of-range) so user input errors result in a friendly AthenaException instead of uncaught exceptions.

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/419#discussion_r2778679676" expanded>

**10 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/419#discussion_r2778680271" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**11 :fas-comment:**
