</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/247#discussion_r2769812276" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The method name makeFolder is understandable, but Java naming guidelines usually prefer verb phrases that describe intent more explicitly, such as createDataDirectoryIfMissing. This can improve readability and self-documentation.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/247#discussion_r2769823072" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

repo, tasks, and ui are declared as static but are primarily used as instance-level state. From a coding standards and design perspective, consider whether these should be instance fields instead, to avoid shared state and improve encapsulation.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/247#discussion_r2770248108" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The variable name descriptions is used in multiple methods but sometimes represents different things (task description, date input, task number). More specific names (e.g. arguments, taskDetails, dateParts) could improve clarity.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/247#discussion_r2770270108" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The code accesses fields like ((Deadline) t).by, from, and to directly. From a code quality and encapsulation standpoint, using getter methods would be preferable and align better with object-oriented design conventions.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/247#pullrequestreview-3757883751" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**

<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/421#discussion_r2770314221" expanded>

### 285. SONG..YANG `@song-yiyang` (10 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/13#discussion_r2752367673" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

A slight nitpick, but perhaps a simple header comment for this not-as-trivial private method?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/13#discussion_r2752370585" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Not sure I like this old switch-case syntax, perhaps consider the newer enhanced `switch` syntax that can make this code cleaner? Like below:

'''java
return switch (cmd) ``{``
case LIST -&gt; listTasks();
case MARK -&gt; markTaskDone(Parser.parseId(input, 5));
case UNMARK -&gt; markTaskUndone(Parser.parseId(input, 7));
case REMOVE -&gt; removeTask(Parser.parseId(input, 7));
case FIND -&gt; findTasks(Parser.parseKeyword(input, 5));
case TODO -&gt; addToDo(Parser.parseDescription(input, 5));
case DEADLINE -&gt; addDeadline(input.substring(9));
case EVENT -&gt; addEvent(input.substring(6));
default -&gt; "I'm sorry, I don't know what that means.";
``}``;
'''
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/13#discussion_r2752373650" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/432#discussion_r2788681574" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/432#discussion_r2788684817" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

You could make it more specific, e.g., “Parses a todo command and adds a new Todo to the task list, then persists changes.” That makes the method’s side effects (add + save) explicit.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/432#pullrequestreview-3779767692" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**

<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Consider making these fields private final instead of public final to preserve encapsulation. External code should ideally interact via methods (or toString) rather than direct field access.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/463#discussion_r2788703583" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

If desc is defined in Task, you might want to initialize it via super(desc) (and keep desc private in Task) instead of assigning it directly in subclasses. This keeps responsibility for base fields in the superclass.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/463#discussion_r2788706118" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Minor readability suggestion: you could extract the status marker logic into Task (e.g., getStatusIcon()), so subclasses don’t repeat the (isDone ? "X" : " ") formatting.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/463#discussion_r2788707809" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Nice idea with DisplayMessage. You could consider extracting 60 into a named constant (e.g., private static final int LINE_LENGTH = 60) to make the intent clearer and easier to tweak.

### 298. MANI..HARY `@NotDotManish` (9 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/226#discussion_r2778595845" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Returning null for invalid commands makes error handling harder and can lead to NullPointerException later. Consider throwing a DukeException (with a helpful message) instead, so the caller can show a clear user-facing error and keep the control flow explicit.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/226#discussion_r2778596062" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

This split is very sensitive to exact spacing (" DUE: " / " START: "). If the user types extra/missing spaces, parsing may fail unexpectedly. Consider splitting on "DUE:" / "START:" then trim(), or using indexOf + substring (or a regex that tolerates variable whitespace) to make parsing more robust.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/226#discussion_r2778596756" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

These fields look like they can be private final (and accessed via getters if needed) to improve encapsulation and prevent accidental mutation from subclasses.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/226#discussion_r2778597544" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Minor naming/UI clarity: this looks like an event’s end time, so due: might be confusing (it reads like a deadline). Consider changing the label to end: (or to:) for consistency with the event concept.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/226#pullrequestreview-3768907686" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Left a few inline comments on parser robustness and error handling. In particular, noted cases where parsing depends on exact spacing and where null is returned for invalid input, which could lead to harder-to-debug errors. Also left a small UI clarity suggestion regarding event end-time labeling.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/356#discussion_r2778584011" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In the user guide, you might want to use just the product name instead of the package name
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/356#discussion_r2778589417" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Possible test bug: in testParseUnmarkCommand, Parser.parse("unmark 1") is asserted as MarkCommand.class. Should this be UnmarkCommand ? If it’s intentional, might be worth clarifying why unmark returns a MarkCommand.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/356#discussion_r2778589721" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Parsing robustness: rest.split(" /from | /to ") depends heavily on exact spacing. Users might type extra spaces or no spaces around /from /to. Consider parsing via indexOf("/from") / indexOf("/to") and slicing, or use a regex that tolerates variable whitespace (e.g., \\s*/from\\s* etc.). This will reduce format-related false negatives.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/356#pullrequestreview-3768891764" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Left a few inline comments on documentation and parser robustness.
</panel>

</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>

<div slot="header">

ToDo is usually written as Todo (standard CamelCase). Renaming ToDo → Todo will look more consistent with typical Java class naming.

### 321. KARA..USIK `@anishkousik` (9 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/48#discussion_r2779296764" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Consider improving exception handling in the getResponse() method. Currently, 
all exceptions are caught with a generic catch block. It would be better to:
1. Preserve the original exception type information
2. Log the error properly rather than redirecting to stdout
3. Return error messages in a consistent format to the GUI
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/48#discussion_r2779297203" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Minor style issue: The file is missing a newline at the end. 
Add a newline character at the end of the file to follow Java conventions.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/48#discussion_r2779297751" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The image URLs are hard-coded strings. Consider:
1. Moving these to a constants file or configuration
2. Using local resources instead of external URLs to avoid potential network issues
3. Adding error handling in case the images fail to load
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/48#pullrequestreview-3769773067" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/129#discussion_r2779303360" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The handleMark() and handleUnmark() methods call tasks.get(index) without 
checking if the index is within bounds. This could throw an 
IndexOutOfBoundsException if the user provides a valid number that's too large.

Consider adding a bounds check:
if (index &lt; 0 || index >= tasks.size()) ``{``
    throw new MrDuckyException("Task number " + (index + 1) + " does not exist.");
``}``
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/129#discussion_r2779305056" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The magic number '4' in parseTodo() assumes the command is exactly "todo". 
If the substring operation fails or input length is less than 4, this might
throw a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException.

I think you can consider making the code more robust by maybe
1. Extract the command length dynamically using input.indexOf(" ")
2. Add proper bounds checking before calling substring()
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/129#discussion_r2779306176" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

When an IOException occurs during file reading, the error is printed to 
System.out but the method continues and returns whatever tasks were loaded. 
This could lead to data loss without the user realizing it.

Consider improving error handling:
1. Use proper logging instead of System.out.println()
2. Return a Result type or throw a checked exception to force callers to 
   handle the error
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/129#pullrequestreview-3769780343" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/402#pullrequestreview-3761436490" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**9 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, the implementation is well-structured and readable, with solid task management functionality. A few refinements can better align the code with intermediate Java conventions and SE-EDU standards. Avoid catching generic Exceptions. can try to catch specific exceptions such as IOException or IllegalArgumentException
</panel>

</panel>

</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/18#discussion_r2774766915" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The variable name i is used. While the standard allows short names like i for scratch variables or iterators, would a more descriptive name be better here since its scope extends through the method's return statement?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/18#discussion_r2774776126" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Similar to the previous point, I noticed the use of int i in printRemove. Perhaps a more descriptive name would clarify what this integer represents in the context of the task list?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/18#discussion_r2774791226" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the parameter isDone is named to sound like a boolean. I like that you used the prefix 'is', which follows the standard naming convention for boolean variables, great stuff!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/18#pullrequestreview-3763782758" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, there's intentional use of high-quality naming practices. I have left a few specific suggestions of parameter names so that you can be even more descriptive to help future readers understand the intent of the code at a glance, but I've also highlighted where you did the correct things. The consistency is the best part about your code, all the best!! 💃🏽 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/401#discussion_r2774826054" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

In the showMessage and showError methods, the parameter is named msg. According to the naming guidelines, it is better to avoid texting-style spelling and abbreviations. Perhaps using the full word message would make the code easier to read for others.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/401#discussion_r2774836648" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The method listTasks() is a great examples of using verbs for actions. They immediately tell the reader exactly what the method is doing, which is consistent with standard naming conventions!
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/401#discussion_r2774851088" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like how the handleCommand method acts as a central dispatcher for the logic, I basically did the same thing too! But as more commands are added, this method might become quite long. Would it be worth extracting the parsing logic into a separate parsing class? This would keep the Tommy class focused on the high-level application flow. You could also possibly consider using a switch.

That said, if you would actually consider using a switch just a heads up on 2 things: the coding standard recommends no indentation for the case labels and a // Fallthrough comment if you ever leave out a break
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/401#pullrequestreview-3763845531" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**8 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall, the implementation of the Tommy chatbot is robust and the main logic flow is easy to follow. I particularly like the consistent use of verbs for method names, such as listTasks(), which enhances readability and aligns with standard naming conventions. I have suggested expanding abbreviations like msg to follow naming guidelines more closely and considering a separate Parse class to keep the Tommy class focused on high-level applications. Additionally, I included a reminder on switch statement formatting (case indentation and fallthrough comments) should you choose to refactor the command handling later. Great stuff, all the best to you dude!
</panel>

</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>

### 348. BASI..I XI `@basilkuok` (7 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/214#discussion_r2777619468" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

The remove method does not correctly handle the scenario when the user inputs an invalid argument or a task index that is out of range.

The InvalidSyntaxException returns the error message that some fields are not filled in, which is misleading as the expected behavior would be to inform the user that they should input a number or that the index is out of range.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/214#pullrequestreview-3767186328" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

LGTM. The entire codebase looks good. Only a suggested minor correction that might be helpful, regarding the expected behavior in exception handling. It can be helpful to review other functions to ensure they can implement correct error-handling. 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/406#discussion_r2777631690" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think that you should order the arguments in this second method as (description, by, isFullDay, isDone) so that it aligns with the previous method. That way, it is easier for other programmers to identify quickly that the to methods are related, and calling methods that use either become less clunky (since you won't have to shuffle arguments around).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/406#discussion_r2777632466" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/406#discussion_r2777637332" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

For consistency with the rest of the code, global variables that are named in all-caps should be reserved for static variables (like the command parser variable in Parser.java).
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/406#discussion_r2777649726" expanded>

### 352. PHOA..ERYL `@sherylphoa` (7 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2773716794" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like how you kept the code cleaner by defining constants separately and good job following the coding standard for naming constants.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2773743368" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I like how you have different tests for different errors which I also saw in the UnmarkCommandTest. What are some other errors that you can test for? 
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#discussion_r2773766830" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Currently, the DeleteCommand needs to know a lot about the internal structure of TaskList (like its size and how to remove items). Could we improve the abstraction by creating a method in TaskList that handles the deletion process entirely? This way, the command wouldn't need to 'know' how the list is managed? I noticed the same issue in other places as well.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/176#pullrequestreview-3762613423" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED


</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/297#discussion_r2773805473" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I noticed the `run()` method currently handles both the identification of commands and the specific logic for building tasks, which leads to those similar try-catch patterns being repeated for each case. Do you think we could move the responsibility of validating arguments and creating the task objects (like `Todo` or `Deadline`) entirely into the `CommandParser`? That way, the `run()` loop could focus solely on calling a single execute command, making the 'happy path' much easier to read
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/297#discussion_r2773823380" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Using `Random` is an interesting way to test, but it might make it hard to reproduce a bug if the test fails only once in a while. Would it be helpful to use fixed values—like the first item (`1`), a very large number, or even a negative number—to ensure the parser is consistent? Also, instead of using `try-catch` with `fail()`, we could let the test throw the exception or use `assertThrows` to make the test code much cleaner! To make it even more robust, we could also include more 'success' or 'failure' tests for things like empty arguments or non-integers?
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/297#pullrequestreview-3762715630" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**7 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Great work on getting the core functionality of TalkingPal up and running! The command-driven logic is solid and I have added some comments on how you can make it cleaner!
</panel>

</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/385#discussion_r2767962406" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

As per the Java coding standard, "The switch statement should have the following form"

```
switch (condition) ``{``
case ABC:
    statements;
    // Fallthrough
case DEF:
    statements;
    break;
case XYZ:
    statements;
    break;
default:
    statements;
    break;
``}``
```
Perhaps enable this setting in your editor.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/385#discussion_r2767967647" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think it's more common to let Ui class to be only reponsable for displaying response, instead of taking the input-to-response logic. So the classname Ui here may be confusing. My suggestion is moving the input-to-response logic to other classes and keep this class clean for showing/displaying.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/358#discussion_r2753452354" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think this method is in a great need of a javadoc, as there are hundreds of lines of code inside. I would find it's difficult to write a javadoc for these amount of codes. Like writing javadoc, debug, and changing exist features or adding new features.

### 370. ANNA..NNES `@annamacinnes` (5 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/197#discussion_r2772854474" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

I think by convention, boolean methods are recommended to start with words like 'is' 'was' or 'has', so maybe it might be better to change this method name to 'isOn()' or 'isOccuringOn'? 
</panel>

Same thing as your Deadlines occursOn method, I think by convention, boolean methods are recommended to start with words like 'is' 'was' or 'has', so maybe it might be better to change this method name to 'isOn()' or 'isOccuringOn'?
</panel>

### 378. WONG.. REN `@oofyneril` (6 comments)
</div>


<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/9#discussion_r2773045259" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**1 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Storage.readFromFile() can crash on malformed lines

If the line is missing fields (e.g. "TODO | false"), details[2] throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

You don’t catch that exception in readFromFile (you only catch FileNotFoundException and InvalidActionException), so a corrupted save file can crash the whole app.

Validate details.length before indexing, or catch RuntimeException and skip that line would be my recommended fix
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/9#discussion_r2773052925" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**2 :fas-comment:**
</div>

find with no keyword matches everything. For this line, if user types find only, the keyword becomes "".

And in TaskList.find:

if (task.taskName.contains(keyword)) // contains("")

Every string contains "", so it returns all tasks, which is probably not what you want.

If keyword is blank → throw MissingTaskException or show a message. thats my Suggestions
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/9#pullrequestreview-3761681943" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**3 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall,
Command.fromString() is clean and safe.
convertDateTimeString() correctly enforces yyyy-MM-dd HHmm and throws a custom exception.
Tests using @TempDir + passing stubPath is a solid pattern.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/154#discussion_r2773016714" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**4 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Bossa ignores the filePath you pass in
public Bossa(String filePath) ``{``
    ui = new Ui();
    storage = new Storage("data/bossa.txt"); // &lt;-- hard-coded
    ...
``}``

But in Main you do:
private Bossa bossa = new Bossa("data/tasks.txt");


So the parameter is never used. This is almost certainly a bug. Should be:
storage = new Storage(filePath);


Otherwise you’ll get confusing “why is it saving to the wrong file?” behavior.
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/154#discussion_r2773026418" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**5 :fas-comment:**
</div>

Task.fromStorageString has no validation

String[] parts = line.split(" \\| ");
String type = parts[0];
boolean isDone = parts[1].equals("1");
...

If a line is malformed (too short, missing fields), this will throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

You do catch exceptions in Storage.loadTasks(), so it won’t crash
</panel>

<panel  popup-url="https://github.com/NUS-CS2103-AY2526-S2/ip/pull/154#pullrequestreview-3761647667" expanded>
<div slot="header">

**6 :fas-comment:**
</div>

**Review Status:** COMMENTED

Overall,
Deadline / Event date parsing and formatting logic is looks fine
JavaFX wiring (Launcher, Main, MainWindow, DialogBox) looks standard 
Parser.getCommandWord() is OK for simple commands 
mark/unmark/remove logic is reasonable 
</panel>

</panel>

<panel type="info"  collapsed>

is not a valid number (e.g. "delete abc").

PsyduckException for consistent error handling.

Please keep line lengths within 120 characters to adhere to the coding style guidelines. You can separate at the "+" like such:

'''suggestion
	return "[E]" 
	        + super.printTask() 
	        + " (from: " + this.from.format(DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("d MMM yyyy")) 
	        + " to: " + this.to.format(DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("d MMM yyyy")) + ")";
'''
</panel>

Overall, I liked how your code demonstrates excellent naming practices with strong adherence to the guidelines.

## Classes
I think you followed the "noun or noun phrase" guideline for class names very well, such as with Parser.java and TaskList.java.

## Method Names
Your method names also follow the guidelines very well, using clear, descriptive verbs.
`isDone()` and `getDescription()` in Task.java are an example of how you used prefixes to clearly demonstrate your code's intent!
</panel>

'''suggestion
        int isDone;
        try ``{``
            isDone = Integer.parseInt(parts[1].trim());
        ``}`` catch (NumberFormatException e) ``{``
            throw new TankaException("Invalid task status in save file: " + parts[1].trim());
        ``}``
       if (isDone != 0 && isDone != 1) ``{``
           throw new TankaException("Task status must be 0 or 1 in save file, found: " + isDone);
       ``}``
'''

Nice: clean structure and consistent style throughout — easy to follow.
